Commercial Mar 24, 2026

How Much Does Pilot Training Cost Before A US Airline First Officer Reaches The Flight Deck?

A closer look at the roadmap to the cockpit.

The cumulative cost of flight training—including necessary certifications, hours, and education—often exceeds $200,000 before pilots secure their first airline position, creating significant financial barriers that impact pilot supply.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 24, 2026

U.S. Considering Deployment of 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East

Senior officials are reportedly considering the deployment of the Immediate Response Force, which includes a Battalion Combat Team of the 82nd Airborne Division, according to U.S. media. After increasing rumors regarding a deployment of U.S. Army troops being in progress, new reports now mention ...

Military airlift operations may face increased demand as potential 82nd Airborne deployment could strain strategic transport capacity and readiness.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Premium For Everyone: United Airlines Invests In 3 Aircraft Types To Transform Flying

Since 2019, the legacy carrier has added nearly 40% more premium seats.

United's diversified fleet modernization strategy signals that premium capacity expansion is now essential for legacy carriers to compete with ultra-low-cost competitors across all route segments.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

Operators must document their Safety Management System processes and demonstrate continuous compliance to avoid regulatory enforcement action.

Military Mar 24, 2026

How The Fairchild Republic A-10 Warthog’s Titanium Bathtub Protects Pilots From Ground-Level Ballistics

The story behind America's flying tank buster.

The A-10's titanium armor protects pilots during low-altitude close air support missions where ground fire exposure is unavoidable, making survivability through redundant systems critical for sustained operations.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA PC-12 Aircraft Makes Move to Support Flight Research Across Agency

A NASA Pilatus PC-12 aircraft will now be based at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, in order to support flight research efforts across the agency. The PC-12 was acquired in 2022 by NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland for use in advanced technology development....

NASA's relocation of its PC-12 from Glenn to Armstrong enables more efficient multi-center research collaboration by positioning the aircraft at a major flight test facility.

See NASA →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA

Delta Air Lines said on March 24, 2026, that it is temporarily suspending special services for members of… The post Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA appeared first on AeroTime.

Delta's suspension signals how airline operational decisions increasingly respond to government dysfunction, with TSA staffing impacts creating cascading effects beyond security screening.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Air Transat Scraps New 12-Hour Longest Route Before A Single Flight Takes Off

The leisure operator was due to take off from Toronto to Ghana's capital on June 27. But the route has been pulled before the first departure...

Rising fuel costs and demand uncertainty are forcing even established carriers to abandon long-haul leisure routes pre-launch, signaling market contraction in ultra-long-range leisure travel.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Strong safety culture directly reduces incident rates by enabling frontline staff to report hazards without fear, making SMS effectiveness dependent on psychological safety rather than compliance alone.

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

How Much Does It Cost To Upgrade To Lie-Flat Business Class On Transatlantic Flights?

Booking business class isn't the only way to access lie-flat transatlantic aviation. Most major transatlantic airlines offer upgrades.

Strategic upgrade purchasing on transatlantic routes can provide lie-flat access at significantly lower costs than direct business class bookings, creating revenue optimization opportunities for airlines and value opportunities for premium economy passengers.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for satellite execs

A shifting geopolitical landscape is driving huge business opportunities for satcoms but also bringing new supply chain and regulatory challenges, industry executives said March 24 at the Satellite Conference in Washington, D.C. The post Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for s...

Satellite operators must navigate competing national security regulations and supply chain fragmentation as geopolitical tensions create simultaneous market expansion and operational complexity.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Reminders of Where We’ve Been, Where We’re Going

Three Moon rocks are on display during a March 24, 2026, event where NASA announced a series of transformative agencywide initiatives designed to achieve the National Space Policy and advance American leadership in space. NASA leadership provided updates on mission priorities, including sending t...

NASA's integrated space policy initiatives signal a strategic shift toward sustained lunar operations and commercial partnerships that will reshape aerospace supply chains and workforce demands.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

No Transponder, No Warning: NTSB Details LaGuardia Alert Failure

NTSB says LaGuardia’s surface warning system was "unable to alert."

LaGuardia's surface detection equipment failed to trigger alerts for a non-equipped aircraft, exposing critical gaps in ground-based collision avoidance when transponders are absent.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via taxi

Runway exit design at LAX creates systematic confusion for pilots in high-traffic conditions, necessitating proactive hazard mitigation beyond standard procedures.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Expands Swift 100R Engine STC

Amended STC broadens engine coverage for the unleaded avgas.

The expanded STC enables the Swift 100R unleaded fuel to power a broader aircraft fleet, reducing lead emissions across more GA aircraft types without requiring individual supplemental type certifications.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

3 Ways Students Can Get Involved With Artemis

NASA’s Artemis program will establish a sustainable lunar presence, unlock new scientific discoveries, and develop technologies for spaceflight to Mars and beyond – and students can help shape this new era of space exploration. As America launches this new Golden Age of innovation and exploration...

NASA's Artemis program offers educational pathways for students to contribute directly to lunar exploration and deep-space technology development, creating workforce pipeline opportunities for aerospace careers.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision

The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) held a second briefing, on March 24, 2026, that offered further details… The post NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision appeared first on AeroTime.

Complex accident causation patterns like those at LaGuardia underscore the aviation industry's need for enhanced multi-factor risk assessment protocols beyond single-point failure analysis.

General Aviation Mar 24, 2026

NASA Selects Finalists in Student Aircraft Maintenance Competition

Editor’s note: This advisory was updated on March 24, 2026, to update the finalists list. NASA has selected seven student teams as finalists in the 2026 Gateways to Blue Skies Competition, giving them the resources to help address a critical challenge for U.S. aviation: aircraft maintenance. Chal...

NASA's focus on student maintenance training through competition signals the industry's urgent need to address the skilled technician shortage affecting U.S. aviation operations.

See NASA →

General Aviation Mar 24, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use t

Eastern Iowa Airport's mixed-use model supporting flight training alongside commercial operations demonstrates a viable strategy for regional airports to maintain financial sustainability while developing pilot talent.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

ResilienX Receives FAA Waiver for Remote BVLOS Operations

ResilienX, Inc., a leader in aviation safety and BVLOS drone networks, announced today that it

FAA waiver enables ResilienX to conduct beyond-visual-line-of-sight operations without visual observers, accelerating commercial drone deployment for infrastructure and emergency response applications.

Drones Mar 24, 2026

When Wireless Networks Falter, Drones Can Provide Backup Connectivity, According to Stevens Researchers

Researchers at Stevens Institute of Technology have developed a drone-based system designed to provide temporary

Aerial relay drones could restore critical communications during natural disasters or infrastructure failures, creating new operational roles beyond traditional delivery applications.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

America’s new 911 drone is always ready

Drones responding to 911 calls are no longer experimental. Across the US, police departments are already using Drone as First Responder (DFR) programs to get eyes on emergencies faster than patrol units. In many cities, drones arrive in under two minutes, well before officers. But even with that ...

Police drone-first response programs are reducing emergency response times to under two minutes, fundamentally changing how departments prioritize resource dispatch in critical situations.

See DroneDJ →

Air Cargo Mar 24, 2026

How Much Do Cargo Pilots Make Compared To Passenger Airline Pilots?

Passenger airline pilots can earn more, but being a cargo pilot is also a lucrative career.

Cargo pilots often achieve comparable earnings to passenger pilots despite lower seniority requirements, making freight operations an attractive career alternative for aviators seeking faster income growth.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Mar 24, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational efficiency improvements across all aviation layers offer immediate environmental gains while managing capacity growth without proportional infrastructure expansion.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA X-Ray Mission Gets Fresh Look at 2,000-Year-Old Supernova

NASA’s IXPE (Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer) mission has taken a new observation of a supernova, RCW 86, helping fill in a fuller picture of what other telescopes have observed. When astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory previously targeted RCW 86, they discovered that a large “c...

IXPE's polarimetry data on RCW 86 enables multi-wavelength analysis that refines our understanding of supernova physics, informing models for space environment hazards affecting spacecraft systems.

See NASA →

Industry Mar 24, 2026

SparkPNT Enters the Next Chapter with Facet FP

Designed with the belief that users should have total control over the devices they own,

SparkPNT's partnership with Facet FP reflects growing industry demand for user-controlled positioning systems as alternatives to GPS dependency in critical operations.

See sUAS News →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Moog taps Redwire to provide solar arrays for Meteor

WASHINGTON — Redwire announced the first award for its new solar arrays, a $12.8 million contract from Moog. Under the contract announced March 24, Redwire will deliver Extensible Low-Profile Solar Array (ELSA) wings for Moog’s Meteor satellite bus ordered by an undisclosed national security cust...

Redwire's commercial solar array technology is gaining traction in the national security satellite market, signaling growing demand for compact power systems in government space platforms.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: LaGuardia Surface Alert System Did Not Trigger

Investigators said the fire truck involved did not have a transponder, and a stepped-on radio transmission remains under review.

Equipment gaps and communication breakdowns at major airports create blind spots in surface safety systems, highlighting the need for mandatory transponder compliance and clearer radio protocols.

See AVweb →

IAA Prosecutes Drone Operator for Impeding Investigation and Breach of Direction

The Irish Aviation Authority (IAA) brought a prosecution in the District Court against a drone operator for impeding an official investigation

Regulatory enforcement against drone operators is expanding beyond airspace violations to include obstruction of safety investigations, signaling stricter accountability measures.

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Here’s Why Airlines Keep Refurbishing Boeing 777-300ER Cabins Instead Of Replacing Them

Airlines refurbish their Boeing 777-300ER cabins as it offers a more cost-effective solution and improves the efficiency of an aircraft's lifecycle.

Extended cabin refurbishment cycles for 777-300ERs signal airlines' confidence in airframe longevity despite supply chain pressures, delaying fleet modernization investments.

Space-based IoT connectivity breakthroughs could enable airlines to collect real-time aircraft and cargo telemetry globally, reducing communication dead zones during flight operations.

FAA Updates GNSS Interference Guide Months After First Release

Update adds pilot procedures and expands detail on recognizing and reporting jamming and spoofing.

The expanded guidance now equips pilots with actionable procedures to identify and report GNSS threats, transforming reactive incident response into proactive risk management.

El Al cuts capacity by 95% as Israel limits Ben Gurion Traffic

The 95% capacity reduction signals potential cascading effects on El Al's operational costs, crew utilization, and revenue that could force strategic restructuring beyond the conflict period.

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

The Striking Differences Cabin Crew Notice Between The Boeing 787 Dreamliner & Airbus A350

The battle of the long-haul giants.

Cabin crew preferences between these aircraft reveal operational trade-offs: the 787's wider fuselage enables better galley access and passenger movement, while the A350's higher cabin pressure and humidity reduce fatigue on ultra-long routes.
